danger_signals.visual_form: Tension theme music plays when opponent team approaches victory
danger_signals.meaning: Imminent loss state; player must react to prevent defeat

danger_signals.visual_form: Enemy team characters cluster at net for spike opportunity

safety_signals.meaning: Low-pressure training environment; safe learning space

safety_signals.visual_form: Player characters positioned for blocking or counter-attack

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: USA logo on scoreboards; stars and stripes originally on V'Ball logo (removed in international version)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: U.S. Navy team in unique home court setting
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: American circuit courts: Daytona Beach, New York (CHERRY sign), Chicago (PIZZA scoreboard text), Las Vegas (color-cycling neon), Los Angeles (USC/USA logo)

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Soviet team as special match opponent
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Kremlin backdrop (redesigned from imposing tank row in Famicom version to less hostile appearance in NES version)

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: National team designations (Japan, Italy, Australia, Mexico, Brazil)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: World Cup tournament bracket features fixed nationalities in NES version (randomized in Famicom)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Hawaii court serves as World Cup venue; crowd faces slightly modified between versions; ICE CREAM sign (changed from 'Hagodda')

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Navy team as special opponent with unique court
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Naval base court setting; unique visual environment distinct from beach/tropical venues

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Daytona, New York, Chicago, Las Vegas courts with increasing visual complexity and distinct regional markers
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Opponent difficulty increases; team AI becomes more aggressive

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Transition to Hawaii venue; tropical environment; international team representation through court aesthetics and signage
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Music changes for each World Cup match; hard difficulty activated

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Unique specialized courts: naval base and Kremlin backdrop; visually distinct from earlier beach venues
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Highest difficulty tier; tension theme plays more frequently

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Winner's theme music plays; championship scene displayed
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Tournament ends; player returns to main menu for replay or mode selection

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Japanese version emphasizes U.S. identity (stars/stripes); international version removes U.S.-centric imagery; Kremlin replaces tanks to reduce perceived hostility
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Tournament structure differs: Famicom has single difficulty-adjustable tournament; NES splits into three separate courses (Exercise, American Circuit, World Cup) serving as implicit difficulty levels

environmental_storytelling.example: New York court displays CHERRY sign (SAKURA in Japanese), Chicago shows PIZZA (SOBA in Japanese), Los Angeles has USA logo—each reflects regional American identity and local food culture
Las Vegas court uses animated color shifts (red→dark red→purple→light blue in international version) to convey neon nightlife aesthetic distinct from other venues
USSR court originally featured imposing tank row in Famicom; redesigned to Kremlin in NES to reduce militaristic threat perception while maintaining national identity
Hawaii and other courts show crowd faces with minor modifications between versions, suggesting distinct audience composition by region
Daytona court mascot altered between versions; 'LlouorA' logos replaced with 'V'Ball' branding to clarify tournament identity
American Circuit sequence (Daytona→New York→Chicago→Las Vegas→Los Angeles) creates implicit narrative of cross-country tournament journey through visually distinct regional courts

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Characters position at net for blocking; can perform receiving, diving, and counter-spike moves
Clusters near net for spike attempts; tension theme audio triggers; visual indication of imminent win condition
Positions shift to baseline; defensive postures adopted; spike frequency decreases
Visible in background (except Exercise mode); animated audience reaction (faces) varies by court location
Team selection screen displays character pairs with controller assignment arrows/numbers; visual team roster communicated

notes: Super Spike V'Ball communicates world-state primarily through environmental theming and localization choices rather than explicit danger/safety systems. The game's progression manifests through geographic and geopolitical signifiers: American regional courts establish casual beach competition context, while USSR/Navy special matches introduce quasi-militaristic opposition. Difficulty progression is implicit in tournament structure (Exercise→American Circuit→World Cup) rather than explicit HUD elements. Regional differences show how visual communication adapts to audience: Japanese version maintains U.S.-centric patriotic symbols; international version neutralizes these and softens USSR visual presentation (tanks→Kremlin). Female team graphics existence but inaccessibility suggests content censorship or development constraints rather than narrative world-state communication. Character team composition (balance/power/speed/defense archetypes) communicates tactical identity but lacks explicit visual differentiation beyond sprite sets.
